title: Landmark Policies
entries: The Iron Levy: every household owes one child to the standing army by their sixteenth year.
The Disbandment: the old mercenary companies were outlawed overnight, and thousands of trained killers now wander the roads for hire.
The Wall Tax: every subject pays a tithe toward a great frontier wall that is, generations later, still unfinished.
The Oath of Swords: nobles may no longer keep private armies — only the crown may raise troops.
The Veterans' Grant: soldiers who serve twenty years are given frontier land, seeding a belt of loyal, hard border-towns.
The Salt Monopoly: all trade in salt became a crown concern, and smuggling it is now punished as treason.
The Free Roads Act: tolls were abolished on every royal highway — a boon to merchants, a windfall for bandits.
The Coinage Reform: the old coins were called in and restruck lighter, quietly robbing every saver in the realm.
The Guild Charter: the craft guilds were granted monopolies on their trades, and prices have climbed ever since.
The Grain Reserve: a share of every harvest is stored against famine — and skimmed, everyone assumes, by whoever guards it.
The Debt Jubilee: once, all debts were forgiven by a single decree, and the lenders have never trusted the crown since.
The Sumptuary Laws: only the nobility may wear certain colours, furs, and gems, on pain of heavy fine.
The Edict of Tolerance: all faiths were declared equal before the law, to the fury of the old state church.
The Temple Seizure: the great temples' lands were taken for the crown, and the priesthood has plotted quietly ever since.
The Calendar Reform: the holy days were rearranged to suit the harvest, and the devout still keep the old dates in secret.
The Heresy Statutes: certain doctrines were named crimes, and informing on a neighbour became a civic virtue.
The Common Court: any subject may now bring suit against a noble — in theory.
The Poor Laws: the destitute are bound to their birth-parish, which must feed them or answer for it.
The Enclosure Acts: the common grazing lands were fenced and granted to lords, and a generation of villagers lost their living.
The Manumission Decree: a class of the unfree were granted their liberty, and their former masters never forgave the crown for it.
The Census Edict: every soul in the realm must be counted and recorded, which the superstitious take as an ill omen.
The Succession Act: the throne may now pass to a daughter, overturning centuries of custom and one very angry cousin.
The Charter of the City: the capital was granted self-rule, and now answers to its own council as much as to the crown.
The Language Decree: all official business must be conducted in the court tongue, sidelining the old regional speech.
The Closed Borders: foreigners may no longer settle without a writ, and the ports have grown suspicious and slow.
The Marriage Treaty: a royal wedding bound two rival crowns, and half the court still calls it a surrender.
The Tribute Pact: the realm pays a yearly sum to a stronger neighbour to keep the peace, and everyone pretends it is a gift.
The Expulsion: a foreign community was ordered out within a season, and their empty quarter of the city stands half-abandoned still.
The Great Survey: the whole realm was mapped and measured for the first time, and taxes rose to match.
The Founding of the Academy: a royal school was established, gathering the realm's talent — and its secrets — under one roof.
The Lighthouse Levy: every coastal town must maintain a beacon, funded by a tax on cargo.
The Road Corvée: every commoner owes a season's labour to the crown's roads and bridges.
The Printing Grant: the copying of books was freed from the scribes' guild, and dangerous ideas now outrun the censors.
The Widows' Pension: the state provides for the families of the fallen, funded by a levy the living resent.
The Beast Bounty: a price was set on a certain monster's head, and the countryside filled with desperate, unqualified hunters.
The Curfew Bell: none may walk the capital's streets after the evening bell without a lantern and a good excuse.
The Reforestation Edict: for every tree felled, three must be planted, enforced by wardens nobody likes.
The Plague Orders: in times of sickness the afflicted are sealed in their homes — a mercy or a death sentence, depending who you ask.
The Duelling Ban: settling disputes by the sword was outlawed, and the courts now choke on grievances once settled in blood.
The Apprentice Act: no trade may be practised without years of formal apprenticeship, pricing the poor out of the crafts.
The Amnesty: a class of exiles and rebels were pardoned and invited home, and some came back angrier than they left.
The Register of Mages: every spellcaster must enroll with the crown, and the unregistered are hunted.
The Harbour Charter: one port was granted the sole right to trade with the far continents, and it grew rich and arrogant overnight.
The Assize of Bread: the size and price of a loaf are fixed by law, and bakers who cheat are pilloried.
The Royal Wardship: orphaned noble heirs become wards of the crown, their lands quietly drained until they come of age.
The Disarmament: commoners were forbidden weapons of war, and the smiths turned to ploughs and grudges.
The Festival Decree: a public holiday was declared to mark the ruler's reign, and it has outlived three dynasties.
The Workhouse Act: grim houses of labour were built for the destitute, places everyone fears and no one closes.
The Water Rights Act: the rivers were declared crown property, and the millers must now pay to turn their wheels.
The Border Resettlement: whole villages were moved to garrison a contested frontier, and their descendants still call themselves exiles.
The Renewed Fealty: every lord was made to swear loyalty afresh in the capital, and two who refused to come are now at war.
The Mint Act: the right to strike coin was taken from the noble houses and reserved to the crown alone.
The Quarantine of Sorcery: magic was confined by law to licensed sanctums, and back-alley casting became a thriving crime.
The Foundling Charter: abandoned children became wards of the state, raised to fill the army and the mines.
The Toll of the Dead: a tax was laid on every burial, which the poor evade with unmarked graves.
The Open Granaries: in the last famine the crown flung wide its stores, winning the people's love and emptying its treasury.
